name: neg_1
property: new returns Err for valid inputs where 0 < number_of_bits < u32::MAX and number_of_bits % 8 == 0
reason: A trivially-rejecting implementation that always returns Err satisfies all ensures clauses since the Ok branch is conditional
params: number_of_bits: usize, result: Result<Bitmap, Error>

name: neg_1_minimal
property: new returns Err for valid inputs where 0 < number_of_bits < u32::MAX and number_of_bits % 8 == 0
reason: Minimal valid input (8 bits = 1 byte) rejected despite satisfying all validity constraints
params: number_of_bits: usize, result: Result<Bitmap, Error>

name: neg_1_typical
property: new returns Err for valid inputs where 0 < number_of_bits < u32::MAX and number_of_bits % 8 == 0
reason: Typical valid input (256 bits = 32 bytes) rejected despite satisfying all validity constraints
params: number_of_bits: usize, result: Result<Bitmap, Error>

name: neg_1_near_max
property: new returns Err for valid inputs where 0 < number_of_bits < u32::MAX and number_of_bits % 8 == 0
reason: Near-maximum valid input rejected despite satisfying all validity constraints
params: number_of_bits: usize, result: Result<Bitmap, Error>

name: neg_2
property: alloc always returns Err even when the bitmap has free (unset) bits
reason: An implementation that unconditionally returns Err violates liveness when free bits exist
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<usize, Error>

name: neg_2_empty
property: alloc always returns Err even when the bitmap has free (unset) bits
reason: Completely empty bitmap (all bits free) but alloc returns Err instead of allocating
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<usize, Error>

name: neg_2_one_free
property: alloc always returns Err even when the bitmap has free (unset) bits
reason: Exactly one free bit remains but alloc returns Err instead of allocating it
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<usize, Error>

name: neg_2_half_free
property: alloc always returns Err even when the bitmap has free (unset) bits
reason: Half the bits are free but alloc returns Err instead of allocating any of them
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<usize, Error>

name: neg_3
property: alloc always returns index 0 regardless of bitmap state
reason: Spec allows starvation of higher-indexed bits since it does not require fairness or progress across indices
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<usize, Error>

name: neg_3_empty
property: alloc always returns index 0 regardless of bitmap state
reason: Empty bitmap trivially satisfies returning 0 since bit 0 is always free — no diversity required
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<usize, Error>

name: neg_3_bit0_free_many_free
property: alloc always returns index 0 regardless of bitmap state
reason: Many free bits available but alloc always picks index 0, starving higher indices
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<usize, Error>

name: neg_3_bit0_set
property: alloc always returns index 0 regardless of bitmap state
reason: Bit 0 is already set so returning Ok(0) violates the precondition that the allocated bit was previously unset — control case
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<usize, Error>

name: neg_4
property: alloc flips or clears bits other than the allocated index
reason: Alloc could set the target bit but silently corrupt other bits if the frame condition is missing
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<usize, Error>, index: usize

name: neg_4_empty
property: alloc flips or clears bits other than the allocated index
reason: No other bits set initially — clearing others is vacuously harmless but setting extra bits is detectable
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<usize, Error>, index: usize

name: neg_4_some_set
property: alloc flips or clears bits other than the allocated index
reason: Other bits are set — flipping them would lose allocated resources or corrupt bitmap state
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<usize, Error>, index: usize

name: neg_4_almost_full
property: alloc flips or clears bits other than the allocated index
reason: Almost full bitmap — flipping a set bit would create a spurious free slot and corrupt allocation tracking
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<usize, Error>, index: usize

name: neg_5
property: set returns Err for a valid in-range index where the bit is currently clear
reason: An implementation could return Err for any input and the Ok branch holds vacuously — liveness gap
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, index: usize, result: Result<(), Error>

name: neg_5_index_zero
property: set returns Err for a valid in-range index where the bit is currently clear
reason: Simplest valid case — index 0, bit clear — but set returns Err anyway
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, index: usize, result: Result<(), Error>

name: neg_5_boundary
property: set returns Err for a valid in-range index where the bit is currently clear
reason: Boundary index (last valid bit) is clear but set returns Err — tests edge of valid range
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, index: usize, result: Result<(), Error>

name: neg_5_already_set
property: set returns Err for a valid in-range index where the bit is currently clear
reason: Control case — bit already set, so Err is the correct behavior per spec; should be SAT
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, index: usize, result: Result<(), Error>

name: neg_6
property: clear succeeds (returns Ok) on a bit that is already cleared, without the spec prohibiting it
reason: Clearing an already-clear bit should fail, but the Ok ensures lack a pre@.is_bit_set guard, allowing spurious success that corrupts usage count
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<(), Error>, index: usize

name: neg_6_already_clear
property: clear succeeds (returns Ok) on a bit that is already cleared, without the spec prohibiting it
reason: Index is valid and bit is already clear; Ok would decrement usage incorrectly since nothing was actually cleared
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<(), Error>, index: usize

name: neg_6_bit_set
property: clear succeeds (returns Ok) on a bit that is already cleared, without the spec prohibiting it
reason: The spec accepts Ok identically whether the bit was set or not, so callers have no spec-level guarantee that clearing actually toggled the bit
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<(), Error>, index: usize

name: neg_6_all_clear
property: clear succeeds (returns Ok) on a bit that is already cleared, without the spec prohibiting it
reason: With usage already at 0 and all bits clear, returning Ok would decrement usage to -1, a logically impossible state
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<(), Error>, index: usize

name: neg_7
property: alloc_range with size == num_bits on a completely empty bitmap could return Err despite sufficient contiguous free space
reason: Returning Err when a valid contiguous free range exists violates the allocator's liveness obligation
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<usize, Error>, size: usize

name: neg_7_all_free
property: alloc_range with size == num_bits on a completely empty bitmap could return Err despite sufficient contiguous free space
reason: A fully empty bitmap has the maximal contiguous free range (0..num_bits), so requesting size == num_bits must succeed with start == 0
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<usize, Error>, size: usize

name: neg_7_minimal
property: alloc_range with size == num_bits on a completely empty bitmap could return Err despite sufficient contiguous free space
reason: A single-bit request on a completely empty bitmap has trivially sufficient free space; Err would indicate a spurious failure
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<usize, Error>, size: usize

name: neg_7_exact_space
property: alloc_range with size == num_bits on a completely empty bitmap could return Err despite sufficient contiguous free space
reason: When exactly enough contiguous free space exists at some location, Err incorrectly claims no valid range is available
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<usize, Error>, size: usize

name: neg_8
property: from_raw_array ensures clause is unconditionally Ok (no Err branch), making the postcondition vacuously true if it ever returns Err
reason: The ensures only constrains the Ok branch, so returning Err trivially satisfies the postcondition, breaking the intended liveness guarantee
params: array: RawArray<u8>, result: Result<Bitmap, Error>

name: neg_8_minimal
property: from_raw_array ensures clause is unconditionally Ok (no Err branch), making the postcondition vacuously true if it ever returns Err
reason: A single-byte array trivially satisfies all preconditions with no overflow risk, yet Err would be vacuously accepted by the spec
params: array: RawArray<u8>, result: Result<Bitmap, Error>

name: neg_8_near_max
property: from_raw_array ensures clause is unconditionally Ok (no Err branch), making the postcondition vacuously true if it ever returns Err
reason: Near u32::MAX sizes are where the runtime checked_mul guard fires, and the spec vacuously accepts Err without flagging the liveness violation
params: array: RawArray<u8>, result: Result<Bitmap, Error>

name: neg_8_all_zeros
property: from_raw_array ensures clause is unconditionally Ok (no Err branch), making the postcondition vacuously true if it ever returns Err
reason: An all-zero array of moderate size should trivially construct an empty bitmap; Err here is pure spec vacuity
params: array: RawArray<u8>, result: Result<Bitmap, Error>

name: gap_1
property: The body implements a next-fit (circular first-fit from `next_free`) allocation policy, but the spec only says some free range is returned without specifying which one is chosen when multiple contiguous free ranges exist.
reason: The spec permits any valid free range, allowing non-deterministic allocation when multiple ranges exist, while the implementation deterministically uses next-fit
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<usize, Error>, size: usize, alt_start: usize

name: gap_1_multi_free
property: The body implements a next-fit (circular first-fit from `next_free`) allocation policy, but the spec only says some free range is returned without specifying which one is chosen when multiple contiguous free ranges exist.
reason: With two valid free ranges, the spec allows returning the later range even if next-fit would have chosen the earlier one
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<usize, Error>, size: usize, chosen: usize

name: gap_1_before_after
property: The body implements a next-fit (circular first-fit from `next_free`) allocation policy, but the spec only says some free range is returned without specifying which one is chosen when multiple contiguous free ranges exist.
reason: When free ranges exist both before and after next_free, the spec allows returning the earlier one, contradicting the circular scan order
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<usize, Error>, size: usize, chosen: usize, midpoint: usize

name: gap_1_wrap
property: The body implements a next-fit (circular first-fit from `next_free`) allocation policy, but the spec only says some free range is returned without specifying which one is chosen when multiple contiguous free ranges exist.
reason: The only range reachable by wrapping is at position 0, but the spec also permits the range at the end, breaking next-fit semantics
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<usize, Error>, size: usize, chosen: usize

name: gap_2
property: After a successful allocation at position `start` with size `size`, the body updates `self.next_free = start + size`. This cursor advancement is not captured in the spec, so callers cannot reason about allocation locality across successive calls.
reason: Without capturing cursor advancement, the spec allows the next allocation to go anywhere, destroying allocation locality guarantees
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<usize, Error>, size: usize, start: usize

name: gap_2_middle
property: After a successful allocation at position `start` with size `size`, the body updates `self.next_free = start + size`. This cursor advancement is not captured in the spec, so callers cannot reason about allocation locality across successive calls.
reason: After allocating from the middle, the cursor should advance past the block, but the spec-visible post-state is indistinguishable from one with cursor at 0
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<usize, Error>, size: usize, start: usize

name: gap_2_near_end
property: After a successful allocation at position `start` with size `size`, the body updates `self.next_free = start + size`. This cursor advancement is not captured in the spec, so callers cannot reason about allocation locality across successive calls.
reason: When next_free reaches num_bits after a tail allocation, the next alloc must wrap to 0, but this cursor state is invisible in the spec
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<usize, Error>, size: usize, start: usize

name: gap_2_sequential
property: After a successful allocation at position `start` with size `size`, the body updates `self.next_free = start + size`. This cursor advancement is not captured in the spec, so callers cannot reason about allocation locality across successive calls.
reason: On a fully empty bitmap the implementation allocates from position 0, but the spec allows a non-zero start, preventing callers from relying on contiguous sequential allocation
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, result: Result<usize, Error>, size: usize, start: usize

name: gap_3
property: When clearing a bit at `index < self.next_free`, the body updates `self.next_free = index`, lowering the allocation cursor to promote reuse of freed low-index slots. The spec says nothing about this state update.
reason: Spec allows stale next_free after clear, breaking allocator reuse of freed low-index slots.
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, index: usize, result: Result<(), Error>

name: gap_3_index_lt_next_free
property: When clearing a bit at `index < self.next_free`, the body updates `self.next_free = index`, lowering the allocation cursor to promote reuse of freed low-index slots. The spec says nothing about this state update.
reason: Freed slot below cursor is invisible to future alloc calls when next_free is not lowered.
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, index: usize, result: Result<(), Error>

name: gap_3_index_geq_next_free
property: When clearing a bit at `index < self.next_free`, the body updates `self.next_free = index`, lowering the allocation cursor to promote reuse of freed low-index slots. The spec says nothing about this state update.
reason: Spec permits spurious cursor changes even when body preserves next_free for index >= cursor.
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, index: usize, result: Result<(), Error>

name: gap_3_lowest_set_bit
property: When clearing a bit at `index < self.next_free`, the body updates `self.next_free = index`, lowering the allocation cursor to promote reuse of freed low-index slots. The spec says nothing about this state update.
reason: Freeing the lowest-index occupied bit without lowering cursor prevents optimal reuse by next alloc.
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, index: usize, result: Result<(), Error>

name: gap_4
property: The spec lacks a liveness (completeness) guarantee: it does not promise that valid inputs (non-zero, < u32::MAX, multiple of 8) produce Ok. Contrast with `from_raw_array`, which explicitly guarantees `result matches Ok(bitmap) && { ... }` given preconditions.
reason: Valid inputs cannot be proven to produce Ok, making verified callers unable to rely on successful construction.
params: number_of_bits: usize, result: Result<Bitmap, Error>

name: gap_4_memory_exhausted
property: The spec lacks a liveness (completeness) guarantee: it does not promise that valid inputs (non-zero, < u32::MAX, multiple of 8) produce Ok. Contrast with `from_raw_array`, which explicitly guarantees `result matches Ok(bitmap) && { ... }` given preconditions.
reason: Memory allocation failure for valid inputs is unaddressed by spec, conflated with invalid-input errors.
params: number_of_bits: usize, result: Result<Bitmap, Error>

name: gap_4_small_valid
property: The spec lacks a liveness (completeness) guarantee: it does not promise that valid inputs (non-zero, < u32::MAX, multiple of 8) produce Ok. Contrast with `from_raw_array`, which explicitly guarantees `result matches Ok(bitmap) && { ... }` given preconditions.
reason: Even minimal valid inputs (8 bits) cannot be proven to succeed, blocking verified construction of any bitmap.
params: number_of_bits: usize, result: Result<Bitmap, Error>

name: gap_5
property: The `set` function does NOT update `next_free`, unlike `alloc_range`. This means manually setting a bit at position `next_free` makes the allocation cursor stale — a subsequent `alloc` will scan past the now-occupied position. The spec doesn't capture this asymmetry.
reason: Spec permits arbitrary next_free changes after set, hiding the cursor-staleness asymmetry with alloc_range.
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, index: usize, result: Result<(), Error>

name: gap_5_index_eq_next_free
property: The `set` function does NOT update `next_free`, unlike `alloc_range`. This means manually setting a bit at position `next_free` makes the allocation cursor stale — a subsequent `alloc` will scan past the now-occupied position. The spec doesn't capture this asymmetry.
reason: Cursor becomes stale pointing to a now-occupied bit; subsequent alloc must scan past it unnecessarily.
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, index: usize, result: Result<(), Error>

name: gap_5_alloc_advanced
property: The `set` function does NOT update `next_free`, unlike `alloc_range`. This means manually setting a bit at position `next_free` makes the allocation cursor stale — a subsequent `alloc` will scan past the now-occupied position. The spec doesn't capture this asymmetry.
reason: Spec doesn't guarantee next_free frame preservation through set, preventing reasoning about cursor stability.
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, index: usize, result: Result<(), Error>

name: gap_5_alternating
property: The `set` function does NOT update `next_free`, unlike `alloc_range`. This means manually setting a bit at position `next_free` makes the allocation cursor stale — a subsequent `alloc` will scan past the now-occupied position. The spec doesn't capture this asymmetry.
reason: Interleaving set and alloc makes cursor behavior unpredictable since spec doesn't constrain next_free through set.
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, index: usize, result: Result<(), Error>

name: gap_6
property: The body returns distinct ErrorCode values depending on the failure reason — `InvalidArgument` for size==0 or size > number_of_bits, and `OutOfMemory` for capacity exceeded or no free range found — but the spec only says `Err(_)`, discarding the error code.
reason: Spec's Err(_) wildcard discards error codes, preventing caller-side failure-reason dispatch.
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, size: usize, result: Result<usize, Error>

name: gap_6_precondition_violation
property: The body returns distinct ErrorCode values depending on the failure reason — `InvalidArgument` for size==0 or size > number_of_bits, and `OutOfMemory` for capacity exceeded or no free range found — but the spec only says `Err(_)`, discarding the error code.
reason: Unverified callers passing invalid size get no spec-level error code information whatsoever.
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, size: usize, result: Result<usize, Error>

name: gap_6_capacity_exceeded
property: The body returns distinct ErrorCode values depending on the failure reason — `InvalidArgument` for size==0 or size > number_of_bits, and `OutOfMemory` for capacity exceeded or no free range found — but the spec only says `Err(_)`, discarding the error code.
reason: Capacity-exceeded OutOfMemory is indistinguishable from scan-failure OutOfMemory in the spec.
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, size: usize, result: Result<usize, Error>

name: gap_6_fragmented
property: The body returns distinct ErrorCode values depending on the failure reason — `InvalidArgument` for size==0 or size > number_of_bits, and `OutOfMemory` for capacity exceeded or no free range found — but the spec only says `Err(_)`, discarding the error code.
reason: Fragmentation-caused failure is conflated with capacity-caused failure under the same Err(_) wildcard.
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, size: usize, result: Result<usize, Error>

name: gap_7
property: The body performs an O(1) early-exit capacity check (`usage > number_of_bits - size`) before scanning, proving via lemma that no contiguous free range can exist when total free bits are insufficient. The spec's Err postcondition (`!exists_contiguous_free_range`) doesn't distinguish this fast-path from a full scan failure.
reason: Spec lacks the simple arithmetic capacity check, forcing callers to reason through the harder existential.
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, size: usize, result: Result<usize, Error>

name: gap_7_full
property: The body performs an O(1) early-exit capacity check (`usage > number_of_bits - size`) before scanning, proving via lemma that no contiguous free range can exist when total free bits are insufficient. The spec's Err postcondition (`!exists_contiguous_free_range`) doesn't distinguish this fast-path from a full scan failure.
reason: Full bitmap is trivially detected by capacity check but spec still requires the existential proof.
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, size: usize, result: Result<usize, Error>

name: gap_7_one_short
property: The body performs an O(1) early-exit capacity check (`usage > number_of_bits - size`) before scanning, proving via lemma that no contiguous free range can exist when total free bits are insufficient. The spec's Err postcondition (`!exists_contiguous_free_range`) doesn't distinguish this fast-path from a full scan failure.
reason: One-bit-short case is O(1)-decidable by capacity but spec demands the full existential witness.
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, size: usize, result: Result<usize, Error>

name: gap_7_fragmented
property: The body performs an O(1) early-exit capacity check (`usage > number_of_bits - size`) before scanning, proving via lemma that no contiguous free range can exist when total free bits are insufficient. The spec's Err postcondition (`!exists_contiguous_free_range`) doesn't distinguish this fast-path from a full scan failure.
reason: Spec merges scan-based failure with capacity-based failure, hiding the body's two distinct proof strategies.
params: pre: Bitmap, post: Bitmap, size: usize, result: Result<usize, Error>
